lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: It is Othello’s pleasure, our noble and valiant general, that, upon certain

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: tidings now arrived, importing the mere perdition of the Turkish fleet, every

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: man put himself into triumph; some to dance, some to make bonfires,

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: each man to what sport and revels his addiction leads him: for,

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: besides these beneficial news, it is the celebration of his nuptial. So

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: much was his pleasure should be proclaimed. All offices are open, and

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: there is full liberty of feasting from this present hour of five

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: till the bell have told eleven. Heaven bless the isle of Cyprus

stage_directions.text: Enter a Herald with a proclamation; People following.

glosses.definition: the Ottoman navy, destroyed by the storm
festival fires built for public rejoicing
kitchen and pantry; places where provisions are kept

annotations.title: The herald's proclamation as relief hinge
annotations.body: This is the shortest scene in the play — nine lines announcing the double cause for celebration (Turkish destruction, Othello's nuptials) and inviting the garrison to revel. Its brevity is the point. Between the dense verse of 2.1 and the long scene of Cassio's humiliation that follows, Shakespeare inserts an oasis of official joy. Structurally it is the exhalation before the drink. It also licenses what comes next: the scene wouldn't work if Cassio weren't out drinking on state occasion, so the state has to announce the occasion.
sources: Harold C. Goddard, *The Meaning of Shakespeare*, vol. 2 (1951)

trials.prompt: Why does the herald proclaim a general holiday in Cyprus?
options: Othello's birthday and ascension to governor of Cyprus
The Turkish fleet's destruction and Othello's wedding celebration
A religious festival commemorating Saint Mark of Venice
The arrival of supplies from Venice after a long siege

trials.prompt: Why did Shakespeare make this scene only nine lines long?
options: He ran out of paper and stopped writing
To provide a brief official transition between Iago's plotting in 2.1 and the drunken confusion of 2.3
To give actors a rest between long scenes
To fulfill a contractual requirement of a minimum twenty scenes
